posts.title: Balistic - Advanced ballistic fire control simulator

comments.content:
Did almost this exact thing on a civil defense training tool a few years ago, and the terrain masking was the hair-puller. SRTM at 90 m looks fine until you try to do horizon scans; 72 rays left weird fan artifacts in tight valleys. We ended up adaptively oversampling azimuth where slope variance spiked and keeping a running max elevation angle per ray. Also, mind the geoid vs ellipsoid mix. If your LOS uses WGS84 heights against EGM96 SRTM, you get phantom cliffs. Classic miss

We also did the Flask + .NET + Redis Streams split. It worked, but the serialization and tile sharding logic ate all the time we thought we saved. The Redis stream became the throughput limiter once we pushed to thousands of impacts. A single in-process library with memory-mapped SRTM tiles beat it by a wide margin. Before you scale out, try a monolith with a tight raster cache and SIMD line-of-sight; you may not need the microservice overhead.

comments.content: You're right to note the throughput limitations of microservices here. That distributed architecture optimizes for horizontal scalability and independent language choices, but inherently sacrifices latency and raw computational efficiency due to serialization and network hops. The monolithic approach with memory-mapped tiles optimizes for sheer speed by minimizing overhead and leveraging SIMD, which is critical for intensive operations like horizon scans across thousands of points. However, it trades away the deployment flexibility and language specialization the microservices model provides. Your experience underscores that for pure computational density, a well-tuned monolith often beats distributed overhead.

posts.title: Facts about fermentation and human consumption
posts.text:
Most humans who consume alcoholic drinks do so not for drink, but for alcohol. This is made possible through careful processes of purifying yeast. You can drink ethanol as a pure chemical that doesn’t have any taste, but is just as intoxicating as any form of alcohol.

When fermenting with SCOBY or other forms of probiotics, you are producing an alcohol that doesn’t make you drunk. It is much more accessible to home brewers, and also more nutritious, but it’s still an alcohol.

This is where we hit a philosophical and biological gray area.

It is true that there is not much, if any, yeast left in your fermented beverage. Also, the alcohol molecule that you see on the periodic table is ethanol, whether produced by yeast or bacteria.

Nevertheless, your fermented beverage is still an alcohol. This means that while fermentation is a form of probiotics, it also produces alcohol.

Alcohol has some great benefits. It can be added to many delicious foods, like vinegar and fruit preserves. It can also be turned into desserts, like beer ice cream, which has the added benefit of being a low-carb, low-fat option.

However, it can also be dangerous and habit-forming. Some people are genetically unable to process alcohol. In these cases, avoiding alcohol is essential for maintaining health. Other people may have a genetic inclination toward alcoholism and require strict monitoring to avoid becoming dependent.

For these reasons, you should be aware that alcohol does come from the fermentation of probiotics. If you have a friend or family member who struggles with their health in this area, you can keep them accountable with your own beverage choice.

If alcohol is a problem, consider what options you have in terms of fermenting your own probiotics. These choices will allow you to enjoy many of the health benefits of probiotics with far fewer concerns about your end product.

## Final Thoughts

If you’ve ever wondered if kombucha or kefir is fermenting probiotics, you now have your answer. These beverages certainly do, and many more besides.

Many foods and drinks can be fermented. Fermentation allows us to make use of microorganisms in a way that changes the nutrient profile of our food. For foods that contain less nutritional value, fermentation makes them more digestible and increases their value. Fermentation also adds to their flavor.

What's different here from resources like Sandor Katz's books or standard microbiology explanations, and does it add any novel insights beyond the philosophical gray area?

comments.content:
This perfectly encapsulates our culture's confused relationship with substances and health optimization. We've turned fermentation into another wellness trend, complete with the familiar dance of claiming benefits while hedging against risks. The whole discourse reads like someone trying to justify their kombucha habit to a recovering alcoholic friend.

What strikes me is how this represents a broader pattern of technologizing ancient practices while losing their cultural context. Traditional societies didn't ferment foods because they were chasing probiotics or worrying about alcohol content on a molecular level. They did it for preservation, flavor, and because the knowledge was passed down through generations who understood the rhythms and purposes intuitively.

Now we dissect every aspect through the lens of optimization and risk management, turning something as fundamental as fermented foods into another category of things to overthink. The irony is that by the time we've analyzed all the variables and consulted our genetic predispositions, we've probably stressed ourselves out more than any trace amounts of ethanol could harm us. Sometimes I wonder if our ancestors were healthier not because their fermented foods were better, but because they didn't spend mental cycles calculating the wellness implications of every sip.
